title: What Your June 30 Financials Are Really Telling a Future Buyer
linkedin:
Most business owners glance at their June 30 accounts once before filing them away.

A buyer will spend weeks with those same three years of figures.

What they build from that review is a picture the seller rarely gets to see: which profit is real, which is owner-adjusted, how cash moves through the year, and whether the business has been run for lifestyle or for value. That picture sets the ceiling on the multiple before any price conversation starts.

The number that gets multiplied is not the figure on your profit and loss. It is normalised EBITDA: earnings after removing items tied to the current owner's circumstances. Owner salary, personal expenses run through the business, genuine one-off costs. A business reporting $300,000 in profit might normalise to $420,000 with clean add-backs applied. At a five-times multiple, that is a $600,000 difference in enterprise value.

The multiple itself is a reward for predictability. Buyers look for three things: revenue that is consistent and growing, cash conversion that tracks the reported profit, and earnings that are believable without the owner present.

Owner dependence is often the most significant and least visible discount. Where the owner holds the key customer relationships and most operational decisions, buyers compensate with a lower multiple. The gap between an owner-dependent business and one with a capable management layer can represent two to three times annual profit.

June 30 is still weeks away. The accounts that matter most to any future buyer are still taking shape. There is still time to influence what they show.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

google:
What do three years of accounts tell a potential buyer about your business?

For most owner-led SMEs in Queensland and across the East Coast, the EOFY accounts are prepared after June 30, reviewed briefly, filed with the ATO, and then set aside. A buyer approaches those same three years very differently. They are looking for a consistent earnings pattern, how well cash converts to profit, and whether the business would keep performing without the current owner.

The number buyers apply a multiple to is not the raw profit figure. It is normalised EBITDA, adjusted for owner salary at market rate, personal expenses run through the business, and genuine one-off items. Each defensible add-back lifts the enterprise value. A business with $300,000 in reported profit might normalise to $420,000 with clean add-backs applied, and at a five-times multiple that is a $600,000 difference in the sale outcome.

June 30 is still a few weeks away. The accounts that will matter most in any future sale are still taking shape, and there are practical steps available right now that influence what those financials show.

If you have been thinking about what your business is worth, the weeks around EOFY are the right time to get a clear picture. ProfitPulse works with Brisbane and East Coast SME owners at exactly this point in the cycle.
